1: μὴ ταρασσέσθω ὑμῶν ἡ καρδία — "Let not your heart be troubled." The verb tarasséō (G5015) is present passive imperative — "stop being troubled," or "do not continue in the state of being shaken." The same verb appears in 5:7 of the stirred pool, and in 13:21 where Jesus himself is described as "troubled in spirit." The command is not to suppress emotion but to redirect trust: the next clause sets up the ground — "believe in God; also believe in me." The conjunction kai ("also") links belief in God and belief in Jesus as parallel acts, not sequential ones.
2:
ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ τοῦ πατρός μου μοναὶ πολλαί εἰσιν — "In my Father's house there are many dwelling-places." Monē (G3438) — from menō (to remain) — is a place of stable, enduring residence, not a temporary lodging. The cognate connection matters: the "dwelling-place" Jesus promises is the same root as the "remaining" (μένω) that structures the vine discourse in chapter 15.

εἰ δὲ μή — the conditional clause is difficult. The Greek reads: "if it were not so, I would have told you." The Evangelist presents Jesus as affirming the Father's house as a known reality, not a speculative hope. The purpose clause ἑτοιμάσαι τόπον ὑμῖν ("to prepare a place for you") uses aorist infinitive — a decisive, completed preparation, not an ongoing process.
3: καὶ ἐὰν πορευθῶ καὶ ἑτοιμάσω τόπον ὑμῖν, πάλιν ἔρχομαι — the two aorist subjunctives (poreutho, hetoimasō) express the contingent departure-and-preparation; then the present indicative érchomai ("I am coming") shifts the return to a certain, already-in-motion reality. The present is used as a definite future: the return is so certain it is stated as a present fact. παραλήμψομαι ὑμᾶς πρὸς ἐμαυτόν — "I will take you to myself" — the verb paralambánō carries the sense of active reception, like taking someone by the hand to bring alongside.
4: καὶ ὅπου ἐγὼ ὑπάγω οἴδατε τὴν ὁδόν — "And where I am going, you know the way." The emphatic egō ("I") separates the subject from the verb. Oídate (G1492, perfect of eidon) carries the force of "you have come to know and it stands known." Thomas's immediate objection in v5 exposes that their knowledge is more implicit than they realize — the declaration is anticipatory, not descriptive of their current self-understanding.
5: οὐκ οἴδαμεν ποῦ ὑπάγεις — Thomas's reply uses the same verb oidamen (perfect "we have known"), but negated: "we have not come to know." The exchange turns on the same word — Jesus asserts they know the way; Thomas counters they do not even know the destination. The mismatch sets up the I-AM saying of v6: the way is not a route to a known place but a person.
6:
ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ὁδὸς καὶ ἡ ἀλήθεια καὶ ἡ ζωή — the sixth predicated I-AM saying of the Fourth Gospel. Three nouns with articles, all in the nominative, linked by kai … kai: "the way, and the truth, and the life." The article before each noun ("the" way, "the" truth, "the" life) is definitive — not one path among many, not a truth among truths. Hodós (G3598) is way, road, path — the means of movement; alētheia (G225) is truth as unconcealedness, reality as it actually is; zōē (G2222) is life in the Johannine sense — the life of the age to come, already present in the Son (John 1:4).

οὐδεὶς ἔρχεται πρὸς τὸν πατέρα εἰ μὴ δι᾽ ἐμοῦ — "no one comes to the Father except through me." The double-negative pattern of the LXX exclusives: oudeis … ei mē — absolute. The preposition dia with the genitive ("through me") marks agency and mediation, not merely association.
7: εἰ ἐγνώκειτέ με, καὶ τὸν πατέρα μου ἂν ᾔδειτε — a mixed past counterfactual. Egnōkeite is pluperfect of ginōskō (relational, experiential knowing); ēdeite is pluperfect of oida (complete, settled knowledge). The sentence distinguishes two kinds of knowing: if you had truly known me (relational knowing), you would already possess settled knowledge of the Father. From v7b, the counterfactual flips to an affirmation: "from now on, you do know him and have seen him" — present tense, applying the completed work of revelation.
8: κύριε, δεῖξον ἡμῖν τὸν πατέρα — Philip's request uses the aorist imperative deixon — "show us" as a single act. The word deíknymi (G1166) means to make visible, to display. Philip imagines a theophany separable from Jesus — a visible display of the Father as a distinct event. The irony is that the display has been ongoing throughout the ministry. καὶ ἀρκεῖ ἡμῖν — "and it will be enough for us." Arkeō (G714) — to suffice, to be content — implies that what they have seen in Jesus is insufficient; the reply in v9 overturns this assumption.
9:
τοσούτῳ χρόνῳ μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν εἰμι — "such a long time I am with you." The dative tosoutō chronō marks duration — Jesus has been present as a fact of ongoing experience. The perfect-present combination charges Philip's failure to recognize: "so long a time" of seeing (θεωρέω) without perceiving.

ὁ ἑωρακὼς ἐμὲ ἑώρακεν τὸν πατέρα — "the one having seen me has seen the Father." Both verbs are perfect tense: heōrakōs (having seen, with the result standing) and heōraken (has seen and it stands seen). The perfect asserts that seeing Jesus is not preparation for seeing the Father later — it is the seeing of the Father, already accomplished.
10: τὰ ῥήματα ἃ ἐγὼ λέγω ὑμῖν ἀπ᾽ ἐμαυτοῦ οὐ λαλῶ — the distinction between two speaking verbs: légō (G3004, to say — the content) and laléō (G2980, to speak — the act of speaking). Jesus says: the things I say I do not speak from myself. The content of his words and the utterance of his speech both originate in the Father. ὁ δὲ πατὴρ ὁ ἐν ἐμοὶ μένων ποιεῖ τὰ ἔργα αὐτοῦ — "the Father who remains in me does his works." Menōn is present participle of menō — the Father's indwelling is ongoing, not sporadic. The works (erga) are the Father's own, performed through the Son.
11: πιστεύετέ μοι … εἰ δὲ μή, διὰ τὰ ἔργα αὐτὰ πιστεύετε — a two-tier appeal to belief, both using present imperative pisteuete. The first tier asks belief grounded in the mutual indwelling claim (v10); the second — "if not, believe for the sake of the works themselves" — appeals to the observable evidence of the works as a lower but valid ground. The pattern recurs from John 10:38: the works testify when the words are not received.
12: ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ τὰ ἔργα ἃ ἐγὼ ποιῶ κἀκεῖνος ποιήσει — "the one believing in me will do the works I do." Present participle pisteuōn — the one who is (continuously) believing. The future poiēsei is a straight promise. μείζονα τούτων ποιήσει — "greater than these will he do." Meizōn (G3187, comparative of megas) — greater in scope and extent, not in kind. The ground for this claim: "because I am going to the Father" — the departure enables the Spirit's coming (John 16:7), and the Spirit's coming enables the mission's worldwide reach.
13: καὶ ὅ τι ἂν αἰτήσητε ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου τοῦτο ποιήσω — "whatever you ask in my name, this I will do." The phrase en tō onomati mou ("in my name") does not mean invoking Jesus as a formula; in Semitic thought, "name" encompasses identity, authority, and character. To ask "in the name of" Jesus is to ask as his representative, in alignment with his character and mission. ἵνα δοξασθῇ ὁ πατὴρ ἐν τῷ υἱῷ — the purpose clause: "so that the Father may be glorified in the Son." The aorist passive subjunctive doxasthē ties answered prayer to the Father's glorification — not a blank check but a shaped promise.
14: ἐάν τι αἰτήσητέ με ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου ἐγὼ ποιήσω — v14 repeats v13 with one addition: the pronoun me ("you ask me") in some manuscripts, making Jesus himself the object of the petition, not merely the name invoked in a petition to the Father. This variant is well-attested (P66, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and shifts the addressee of prayer: in these texts, Jesus explicitly receives petitions made "in his name." The emphasis on repetition in vv13–14 underscores the certainty of the promise.
15: ἐὰν ἀγαπᾶτέ με, τὰς ἐντολάς τὰς ἐμὰς τηρήσετε — the conditional is present subjunctive: "if you love me [as an ongoing disposition], you will keep my commandments." The future indicative tērēsete is not an imperative but a prediction: love issues in obedience naturally. Tēréō (G5083) — to guard, to keep watch over — is stronger than mere compliance; it implies vigilant preservation of what has been entrusted. The possessive tas emas ("my commandments") emphasizes that these are not the Mosaic commands but the commands of Jesus as covenant mediator.
16:
κἀγὼ ἐρωτήσω τὸν πατέρα καὶ ἄλλον παράκλητον δώσει ὑμῖν — the first appearance of παράκλητος (Paráklētos, G3875) in the Gospel. The word is a legal and relational term: one called alongside (para + kaleō) to help, support, advocate, or defend. In Hellenistic legal usage, a paraklētos is a defense advocate in court. Philo uses it of an intercessor with God. The KJV "Comforter" (from Latin comfortare) captures one dimension but misses the legal freight. "Advocate" recovers the forensic register; "Helper" is also defensible.

ἄλλον — "another" of the same kind (G243, allos, not G2087, heteros — another of a different kind). The implication is that Jesus himself has been functioning as Paráklētos for the disciples — 1 John 2:1 makes this explicit. The Spirit is a second Advocate of the same order. εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "forever" — eis ton aiōna, into the age, with perpetuity: the Spirit's presence is permanent in a way that Jesus's physical presence was not.
17:
τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας — "the Spirit of truth." The genitive is appositional: the Spirit who is truth, or the Spirit characterized by truth. Alētheia (G225) in John always carries ontological weight — truth as unconcealedness, the way things actually are before God. The Spirit does not merely teach accurate facts; he mediates genuine reality as opposed to the world's distorted perception.

ὃ ὁ κόσμος οὐ δύναται λαβεῖν — "whom the world cannot receive." Dúnamai (G1410, present indicative) — not merely "does not" but "cannot" — the incapacity is structural, not circumstantial. ὅτι οὐ θεωρεῖ αὐτὸ οὐδὲ γινώσκει — "because it neither sees him nor knows him." Two verbs: theōréō (G2334, perceptual seeing) and ginōskō (relational knowing) — the world lacks both. ὑμεῖς δὲ γινώσκετε αὐτό — "but you know him" — emphatic hymeis ("you" in contrast to "the world"). The present tense suggests the disciples already have this knowing, even if not yet conscious of its full extent.
18: οὐκ ἀφήσω ὑμᾶς ὀρφανούς — "I will not leave you orphans." Orphanós (G3737) — literally, a child bereft of a father; metaphorically, one without a patron or protector in the Greco-Roman world. Orphans were among the most legally and socially vulnerable in antiquity. The word activates both the familial and the social-protection register. ἔρχομαι πρὸς ὑμᾶς — "I am coming to you" — again the present as certain future (cf. John 14:3). The question of whether this refers to the resurrection appearances, the Spirit's coming, or the parousia is debated; the Evangelist may intend the ambiguity to encompass all three.
19: ἔτι μικρὸν καὶ ὁ κόσμος με οὐκέτι θεωρεῖ — "yet a little while and the world sees me no longer." Mikrón (G3397, a little while) — the same phrase frames John 16:16–19. Theōréō (G2334) — extended perceptual seeing. The world's sight of Jesus ends with the crucifixion. ὑμεῖς δὲ θεωρεῖτε με — "but you see me" — same verb, present tense: the disciples' seeing continues past the world's horizon. The basis: "because I live, you also will live." Zō (G2198, present indicative) — "I live" — the resurrection life of Jesus is stated as an ongoing present fact that grounds the disciples' future life.
20: ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ γνώσεσθε — "on that day you will know." The future gnōsesthe (G1097) — the knowing is coming but is not yet complete. "That day" is the day of recognition that comes with the Spirit's arrival and the resurrection. Three mutual indwellings are stated: "I am in my Father, and you in me, and I in you" — a nested mutual indwelling that mirrors the mutual knowing of John 10:14–15 (Father-Son-sheep). The grammar is paratactic: three ens (in) without subordination, all equally real.
21: ὁ ἔχων τὰς ἐντολάς μου καὶ τηρῶν αὐτάς — two participles: "the one having my commands" (echōn) and "keeping them" (tērōn). Possession without keeping is insufficient; keeping without possession is impossible. κἀγὼ ἀγαπήσω αὐτὸν καὶ ἐμφανίσω αὐτῷ ἐμαυτόν — "I will love him and reveal myself to him." Emphanízō (G1718) — to make visible, to disclose, to cause to appear. The verb appears in the Exodus context for God showing his glory (Exodus 33:13 LXX). The self-disclosure of Jesus is conditioned on the love-obedience relationship.
22: τί γέγονεν ὅτι ἡμῖν μέλλεις ἐμφανίζειν σεαυτὸν καὶ οὐχὶ τῷ κόσμῳ — Judas (not Iscariot) raises a reasonable question: if Jesus is going to reveal himself, why not universally? The verb emphanízō from v21 is repeated — the disciples have registered the word. The question implies they expected a public, triumphant Messiah. Jesus's answer redefines revelation: it is relational (love + obedience), not spectacular.
23: ἐάν τις ἀγαπᾷ με, τὸν λόγον μου τηρήσει — "if anyone loves me, he will keep my word." Note the shift from entolás (commandments, plural, vv15, 21) to ton logon mou ("my word," singular): the totality of Jesus's teaching as an integrated whole, not individual commands. καὶ πρὸς αὐτὸν ἐλευσόμεθα καὶ μονὴν παρ᾽ αὐτῷ ποιησόμεθα — "and we will come to him and make our dwelling with him." The plural "we" — Father and Son — is striking; the inhabitation is Trinitarian. Monēn (G3438, dwelling-place) returns from v2: the "many dwelling-places" in the Father's house and the dwelling made in the obedient believer are cognate realities.
24: ὁ μὴ ἀγαπῶν με τοὺς λόγους μου οὐ τηρεῖ — the negative form of v23: "the one not loving me does not keep my words." Present tense throughout — ongoing states. καὶ ὁ λόγος ὃν ἀκούετε οὐκ ἔστιν ἐμὸς ἀλλὰ τοῦ πέμψαντός με πατρός — "the word you hear is not mine but the Father's who sent me." The aorist participle tou pempsantos me — "of the one who sent me" — is one of the Evangelist's characteristic phrases for the Father's commissioning authority (cf. John 5:37; John 7:28; John 8:26). The refusal to receive Jesus's word is thereby a refusal of the Father's word.
25: ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν παρ᾽ ὑμῖν μένων — "these things I have spoken to you while remaining with you." The perfect lelalēka — "I have spoken and the speaking stands" — a summary of the entire Farewell Discourse before it is technically over. Par᾽ hymīn menōn — "while remaining alongside you" — menō again: the physical remaining of Jesus with the disciples is about to end; the Spirit's remaining (v17: menει παρ᾽ ὑμῖν) will replace it.
26:
ὁ δὲ παράκλητος, τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον — the second Paraclete saying. Here the Paraclete is explicitly identified as "the Holy Spirit," and the sending agent shifts: "whom the Father will send in my name." In John 15:26, Jesus sends the Spirit from the Father; here the Father sends the Spirit in the name of Jesus — both are true, the persons of the Trinity acting in coordinated mission.

ἐκεῖνος ὑμᾶς διδάξει πάντα — "that one will teach you all things." Ekéinos is an emphatic demonstrative — "that one" — used to distinguish the Spirit from Jesus as a second distinct person with agency. Didáxei (G1321, future of didáskō) — will teach: the Spirit continues and completes what Jesus began. καὶ ὑπομνήσει ὑμᾶς πάντα ἃ εἶπον ὑμῖν ἐγώ — "and will remind you of all that I said to you." Hypomnēsei (G5279, to remind, to call to memory) — the Spirit's work grounds the apostolic recollection of Jesus's teaching; John's Gospel itself is implicitly presented as the product of this anamnesis.
27:
εἰρήνην ἀφίημι ὑμῖν, εἰρήνην τὴν ἐμὴν δίδωμι ὑμῖν — "Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you." Two verbs: aphíēmi (to release, to send away — the farewell bestowal) and dídōmi (to give — the active gift). Eirēnē (G1515) translates shalom — wholeness, well-being, right relationship, not merely absence of conflict. The possessive tēn emēn — "my peace" — distinguishes this from a generic benediction: it is the specific peace that characterizes Jesus himself, grounded in his relationship with the Father.

οὐ καθὼς ὁ κόσμος δίδωσιν ἐγὼ δίδωμι ὑμῖν — "not as the world gives, I give to you." The world's giving is conditional, transactional, temporary. Jesus's giving is permanent (v16: "forever") and unconditional. μὴ ταρασσέσθω ὑμῶν ἡ καρδία μηδὲ δειλιάτω — the chapter that opened with "do not let your heart be troubled" closes with the same prohibition plus deiliaō (G1167, to be cowardly, to shrink in fear). Two present imperatives: stop being troubled, stop being afraid.
28: ὁ πατὴρ μείζων μού ἐστιν — "the Father is greater than I." This verse is among the most contested in Johannine Christology. Meízōn (G3187, comparative of megas) — greater. The statement follows "I am going to the Father" — the context is missional and relational: the Son going back to the one who sent him, returning to the Father who is the source of the mission. The text does not say "greater in being" but "greater" without specification; interpreters who read this ontologically (Arius) press the text further than its context requires. Within John's own framework (John 1:1; John 10:30; John 20:28), the subordination operative here is functional and economic, not ontological. The note of rebuke — "if you loved me, you would have rejoiced" — grounds Jesus's departure not in loss but in the Father's greatness as the goal of the Son's return.
29: καὶ νῦν εἴρηκα ὑμῖν πρὶν γενέσθαι — "and now I have told you before it happens." The perfect eirēka — "I have spoken and it stands spoken" — is the Evangelist's recurring formula for completed disclosure. The aorist infinitive genésthai — "before happening" — marks the events as temporally future from the moment of speech. ἵνα ὅταν γένηται πιστεύσητε — "so that when it happens, you may believe." The purpose clause shapes the prophetic function of Jesus's words: not merely to inform but to produce belief when the events confirm the prediction.
30: ὁ τοῦ κόσμου ἄρχων — "the ruler of this world." Árchōn (G758) — ruler, chief, magistrate — is used three times in the Gospel for the devil (John 12:31; John 14:30; John 16:11). The title acknowledges real dominion over the present age, while the next clause strips away its power: ἐν ἐμοὶ οὐκ ἔχει οὐδέν — "in me he has nothing." The phrase ouden echei means "has no claim," "has no foothold," "finds nothing to seize." The coming of the ruler is a confrontation, but Jesus meets it without any crack or leverage that evil can exploit.
31: ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα γνῷ ὁ κόσμος ὅτι ἀγαπῶ τὸν πατέρα — "but that the world may know that I love the Father." The purpose of the passion is revelatory: the cross is the demonstration that Jesus loves the Father, enacted in total obedience. καθὼς ἐνετείλατό μοι ὁ πατήρ, οὕτως ποιῶ — "as the Father commanded me, so I do." Eneteilato (G1781, aorist — a definitive commissioning); poiō (G4160, present — ongoing execution of it). The aorist command of the Father and the present obedience of the Son in simultaneous relation. ἐγείρεσθε, ἄγωμεν ἐντεῦθεν — "Rise, let us go from here." Present imperative + hortatory subjunctive. The discourse ends in motion: the theology of the cross is immediately enacted.
